section.title: The Lame Beggar Healed
section.sectionRationale: The Spirit-filled church now bears public witness. Peter and John, going up to the temple, heal a man lame from birth in the name of Jesus. The astonished crowd gathers, and Peter seizes the moment to preach: the power is not their own but comes through faith in the risen Jesus, the Servant, the Author of life, whom they crucified and God raised. He calls them to repent and points to Jesus as the prophet Moses foretold and the offspring in whom all nations are blessed.
section.thesis: In the name of the crucified and risen Jesus, the Author of life, a man lame from birth is made whole, and Peter turns the wonder into a summons to repent and turn to the Servant God raised up, the prophet Moses promised, through whom the blessing of Abraham reaches all the families of the earth.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the healing of the lame man in Jesus' name at the temple gate; Peter's charge that the crowd killed the Author of life whom God raised, and the faith in Jesus' name that healed the man; and the call to repent, with Jesus proclaimed as the promised prophet and the offspring of blessing.

units.label: Healed in the name of Jesus
units.note: in the name of Jesus Christ, get up and walk

verses.text: One afternoon Peter and John were going up to the temple at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour.
And a man who was lame from birth was being carried to the temple gate called Beautiful, where he was put every day to beg from those entering the temple courts.
When he saw Peter and John about to enter, he asked them for money.
Peter looked directly at him, as did John. “Look at us!” said Peter.
So the man gave them his attention, expecting to receive something from them.
But Peter said, “Silver or gold I do not have, but what I have I give you: In the {{onoma|name}} of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, get up and walk!”
Taking him by the right hand, Peter helped him up, and at once the man’s feet and ankles were made strong.
He sprang to his feet and began to walk. Then he went with them into the temple courts, walking and leaping and praising God.
When all the people saw him walking and praising God,
they recognized him as the man who used to sit begging at the Beautiful Gate of the temple, and they were filled with wonder and amazement at what had happened to him.

units.label: You killed the Author of life, but God raised Him
units.note: faith in the name of Jesus made him strong

verses.text: While the man clung to Peter and John, all the people were astonished and ran to them in the walkway called Solomon’s Colonnade.
And when Peter saw this, he addressed the people: “Men of Israel, why are you surprised by this? Why do you stare at us as if by our own power or godliness we had made this man walk?
The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of our fathers, has glorified His {{pais|servant}} Jesus. You handed Him over and rejected Him before Pilate, even though he had decided to release Him.
You rejected the Holy and Righteous One and asked that a murderer be released to you.
You killed the {{archegos|Author}} of life, but God raised Him from the dead, and we are witnesses of this fact.
By faith in the name of Jesus, this man whom you see and know has been made strong. It is Jesus’ name and the faith that comes through Him that has given him this complete healing in your presence.

units.label: Repent and turn: the promised prophet and the blessing of the nations
units.note: times of refreshing and the restoration of all things

verses.text: And now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, as did your leaders.
But in this way God has fulfilled what He foretold through all the prophets, saying that His Christ would suffer.
Repent, then, and turn back, so that your sins may be wiped away,
that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord, and that He may send Jesus, the Christ, who has been appointed for you.
Heaven must take Him in until the time comes for the {{apokatastasis|restoration}} of all things, which God announced long ago through His holy prophets.
For Moses said, ‘The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers. You must listen to Him in everything He tells you.
Everyone who does not listen to Him will be completely cut off from among his people.’
Indeed, all the prophets from Samuel on, as many as have spoken, have proclaimed these days.
And you are sons of the prophets and of the covenant God made with your fathers when He said to Abraham, ‘Through your offspring all the families of the earth will be blessed.’
When God raised up His Servant, He sent Him first to you to bless you by turning each of you from your wicked ways.”

onoma.gloss: Name, and by extension the authority and person named. Peter heals not by his own power but in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth. Throughout Acts the name of Jesus carries His saving authority; it is by faith in that name that the lame man is made whole.

pais.gloss: Servant, or child. Peter calls Jesus God's servant, echoing the suffering Servant of Isaiah whom God glorifies. The title binds the healing and the sermon to the prophecies of the one who would suffer for the people and then be exalted.

archegos.gloss: Author, originator, pioneer, prince. Peter charges that they killed the Author of life, the very source of life, yet God raised Him. The irony is sharp: they put to death the one from whom all life flows, and He now gives strength to a lame man.

apokatastasis.gloss: Restoration, setting all things right again. Heaven receives Jesus until the time of the restoration of all things that God promised through the prophets. It looks to the renewal of creation at Christ's return, the final undoing of the ruin of the fall.

basic.context: After Pentecost, the Spirit-filled church begins to bear public witness. Peter and John go up to the temple at the afternoon hour of prayer. A man lame from birth, who begs daily at the gate called Beautiful, asks them for money. Peter has no silver or gold, but in the name of Jesus he commands the man to walk, and he is instantly healed. The crowd runs together in amazement, and Peter preaches, turning their attention from himself to the risen Jesus and calling them to repent.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the healing as a sign that opens a sermon. The questions to ask are: by whose power was the man healed, what does Peter say the crowd did to Jesus and what God did in response, and what does Peter call them to do? Notice how the miracle is never the point in itself; it is a signpost to the risen Christ and an occasion to call for repentance.
basic.originalLanguages: The man is healed in the onoma, the name, of Jesus, that is, by His authority. Peter calls Jesus God's pais, His Servant, echoing Isaiah, and the archegos, the Author of life, whom they killed and God raised. He points forward to the apokatastasis, the restoration of all things, when Christ returns to make all things new.
basic.typology: Jesus is the prophet like Moses whom God promised to raise up, to whom the people must listen. He is the Servant of Isaiah, glorified through suffering. He is the offspring of Abraham through whom all the families of the earth are blessed. The healed man, leaping and praising God, pictures the restoration the gospel brings.
basic.christ: Peter's message is dense with titles for Jesus: God's Servant, the Holy and Righteous One, the Author of life, the Christ who had to suffer, the prophet like Moses, the offspring of blessing. He was rejected and killed but raised by God, and it is by faith in His name that the man is healed. Christ is the source of both the healing and the salvation Peter offers.
basic.god: The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of the fathers, has glorified His Servant Jesus. God fulfilled through the suffering of Christ what He foretold through all the prophets. God raised Jesus and will send Him again at the restoration of all things. Everything is God's doing, keeping His ancient covenant promises.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 18 is the promise of a prophet like Moses that Peter applies to Jesus. Genesis 22 is the promise that Abraham's offspring would bless all nations. Isaiah 52 and 53 stand behind the title of the suffering Servant. Acts 4 continues the story as the healing leads to opposition and bold testimony.
basic.oneStory: The risen Christ, now reigning, heals through His name and sends His witnesses to call Israel to repentance. Peter shows that Jesus is the fulfillment of everything the prophets promised, from Moses to Abraham, and that in Him the blessing of the nations and the restoration of all things have begun. The gospel that started in Jerusalem is on its way to the ends of the earth.

context.literary: Acts 3 flows directly from Pentecost, giving a concrete example of the wonders and signs mentioned in Acts 2:43. It has two parts: the miracle at the Beautiful Gate and Peter's sermon in Solomon's Colonnade. The sermon parallels Peter's Pentecost address, moving from the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus to a call for repentance, and it leads directly into the arrest and testimony of Acts 4.
context.historical: The setting is the Jerusalem temple at the ninth hour, three in the afternoon, a set time of prayer. The Beautiful Gate and Solomon's Colonnade were well-known parts of the temple complex. A man lame from birth, over forty years old as Acts 4 notes, was a familiar figure, which made the healing undeniable to the watching crowd.
context.canonical: The chapter presents Jesus through a cluster of Old Testament expectations: the suffering Servant of Isaiah, the prophet like Moses of Deuteronomy, and the offspring of Abraham of Genesis. It shows the apostolic pattern of using a sign to proclaim the risen Christ and call for repentance, and it advances the theme of Acts that the blessing promised to Abraham now reaches all the families of the earth through Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the risen Christ at work through His witnesses, healing by His name and calling Israel to repentance, and to present Jesus as the fulfillment of the prophets: the suffering Servant, the prophet like Moses, and the offspring of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular miracle and sermon; it does not promise that every believer will heal the sick on demand or in this manner. The abiding truths are that salvation and wholeness are found in the name of Jesus alone, that the risen Christ fulfills the Scriptures, and that the gospel calls all people to repent and turn to Him.

debates.question: Does the healing of the lame man show that physical healing in Jesus' name is guaranteed for believers today?

views.label: A sign authenticating the apostolic message
views.case: The wonders and signs in Acts confirm the apostles as authorized witnesses of the risen Christ, Acts 2:43 and Hebrews 2:3-4. The healing serves the preaching of the gospel and points to Jesus; it is descriptive of that founding era, not a blanket promise of healing on demand for every Christian.

views.label: A pattern of the kingdom's healing power still available
views.case: Others hold that the same risen Christ still heals through His people, that the name of Jesus retains its power, and that believers should expect and pray for healing as an ongoing expression of the kingdom breaking in, while leaving the outcome to God.

debates.bearing: Both agree the healing points beyond itself to the risen Christ and the salvation offered in His name; the deeper wholeness Peter presses toward is repentance, forgiveness, and the restoration of all things, which no one should reduce to bodily healing alone.

study.originalLanguages: The man is healed in the onoma, the name, of Jesus, by His authority, and it is faith in that name that makes him whole. Peter calls Jesus God's pais, His Servant, the language of Isaiah's suffering Servant whom God glorifies, and the archegos, the Author or Pioneer of life, whom they killed but God raised, the source of life giving strength to a broken body. Heaven holds Him until the apokatastasis, the restoration of all things, the renewal of creation the prophets promised.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus is the prophet God promised to raise up, to whom the people must listen or be cut off.
Jesus is God's pais, the Servant rejected and killed yet glorified by God, fulfilling Isaiah's portrait of the one who suffers for the people.

typology.type: The offspring of Abraham who blesses the nations, Genesis 22:18
typology.fulfillment: In the risen Jesus, sent first to Israel, the promise that all the families of the earth would be blessed through Abraham's offspring begins to come true.

study.christ: Peter piles up titles for Jesus: God's Servant, the Holy and Righteous One, the Author of life, the Christ who had to suffer, the prophet like Moses, the offspring of blessing. They rejected and crucified Him, but God glorified and raised Him, and now heals through His name. The One they killed is the very source of life, and the wholeness given to the lame man is a sign of the fuller restoration Christ brings to all who repent and turn to Him.
study.god: The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God of the fathers, is at work throughout: He glorified His Servant, fulfilled what He foretold through all the prophets, raised Jesus from the dead, and will send Him again at the restoration of all things. God keeps His ancient covenant, sending His risen Servant first to Israel to bless them by turning them from their sins.

crossReferences.note: Moses promised a prophet like himself whom the people must heed; Peter applies this directly to Jesus.
Through your offspring all nations will be blessed; Peter cites this Abrahamic promise as fulfilled in the risen Jesus.
The suffering Servant whom God glorifies stands behind Peter's title for Jesus, God's Servant.
The healing leads to arrest and to Peter's declaration that there is salvation in no other name than Jesus.

study.oneStory: The risen and reigning Christ heals through His name and sends His witnesses to call Israel to repentance. Peter shows that Jesus fulfills the whole line of promise, from Moses to Abraham to the prophets, and that in Him the blessing of the nations and the restoration of all things have begun. The healed man leaping and praising God is a small picture of the wholeness the gospel brings as it moves out toward the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: By what power did Peter heal the man lame from birth?

options.text: By the name of Jesus Christ, through faith in that name.
options.note: Peter is explicit: it is the name of Jesus and faith in Him, not his own power, that healed the man.

options.text: By speaking the name of Jesus over the man.
options.note: The name of Jesus is central, but Peter stresses that it is faith in that name, not a mere formula, that healed him.

options.text: By his own godliness and spiritual power.
options.note: Peter directly denies this, asking why they stare as if by their own power or godliness they made the man walk.

options.text: By the silver and gold he gave the beggar.
options.note: Peter had no silver or gold; he gave what he had, healing in Jesus' name.

quiz.q: What did Peter say the people had done to Jesus, and what did God do?

options.text: They killed the Author of life, but God raised Him from the dead.
options.note: Peter states the sharp contrast: they put to death the Author of life, and God raised Him.

options.text: They handed Jesus over to Pilate to be crucified.
options.note: True, Peter does say they handed Him over, but the striking heart of his charge is that they killed the Author of life whom God raised.

options.text: They misunderstood Jesus but were not really responsible.
options.note: Peter does say they acted in ignorance, yet he still holds them responsible for rejecting and killing Jesus.

options.text: They honored Jesus, and God rewarded them for it.
options.note: The opposite is true: they rejected and killed Him, though God raised Him and still offers them mercy.

quiz.q: What did Peter call the crowd to do in response?

options.text: Repent and turn back, so that their sins might be wiped away.
options.note: Peter's summons is to repent and turn to God for forgiveness.

options.text: Turn from their sins and stop doing wrong.
options.note: Repentance does mean turning from sin, but Peter's call is fuller: turn back to God so sins are wiped away and times of refreshing come.

options.text: Offer sacrifices at the temple to atone for killing Jesus.
options.note: Peter did not point them to temple sacrifice but to repentance and faith in the risen Christ.

options.text: Wait for a future prophet who would explain these things.
options.note: Peter says the promised prophet like Moses has already come in Jesus; the call is to listen to Him and repent now.

quiz.q: How does Peter present Jesus from the Old Testament?

options.text: As the suffering Servant, the prophet like Moses, and the offspring of Abraham who blesses all nations.
options.note: Peter draws these threads together to show Jesus fulfills the whole line of promise.

options.text: As the prophet like Moses whom the people must obey.
options.note: True and cited by Peter, but he also names Jesus the Servant and the offspring of Abraham, a fuller picture.

options.text: As a righteous teacher who kept the law of Moses.
options.note: Peter says far more than this: Jesus is the promised Servant, prophet, and offspring, raised from the dead.

options.text: As a political liberator who would free Israel from Rome.
options.note: Peter presents Jesus as the suffering and risen Christ, not a political deliverer.

quiz.q: What does the healing of the lame man point toward?

options.text: The risen Christ and the fuller restoration of all things He will bring.
options.note: The sign points beyond itself to the reigning Jesus and the coming restoration Peter proclaims.

options.text: The compassion of Peter and John for a poor beggar.
options.note: There is compassion here, but the point is not the apostles' kindness; it is a sign directing the crowd to Christ.

options.text: The power of the risen Jesus at work through His witnesses.
options.note: True and important, but the sign points further still, to the salvation and restoration found in Christ.

options.text: A reward given to the man for his years of faithful begging.
options.note: The healing was a free gift in Jesus' name, not a reward for the man's begging.

questions: Peter had no silver or gold, but he gave what he had in the name of Jesus. What has God given you that is worth more than money to share with someone in need?
The healed man went into the temple walking and leaping and praising God. What has Christ done in your life that makes you want to praise Him openly?
Peter refused to let the crowd credit him, pointing them instead to Jesus. When God works through you, how do you keep the attention on Christ rather than yourself?
Peter told the crowd they had acted in ignorance, yet still called them to repent. How does it comfort you that God meets even our worst failures with a call to turn back to Him?
Jesus is the prophet we must listen to in everything. Is there something Christ is saying to you through His word that you have been slow to obey?
God sent His risen Servant to bless people by turning them from their wicked ways. What would it mean to receive that blessing as a turning, not just a comfort, this week?

translationNotes.note: The ninth hour is explained in a footnote as three in the afternoon, one of the set times of prayer at the temple.
The word rendered servant carries a footnote reading or His child; the term echoes the suffering Servant of Isaiah whom God glorifies, and it recurs in verse 26.
Peter quotes Deuteronomy 18:15 and 18:19 about the prophet like Moses, applying the promise to Jesus.
The promise through your offspring all the families of the earth will be blessed is drawn from Genesis 22:18, the covenant with Abraham.

Footnote markers and line breaks were skipped or treated as spaces when building verse text; the Scripture quotations were joined from their text elements. No em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
